speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This, the sixth conversation with Eugene Settle took place under the willow tree in his backyard in Moscow on August 9th, 1978. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And that's something I was thinking maybe you would remember some about since you were over there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know what the difference was that they mean by that they were just not treated as well. Was it the actual conditions of the camps and the... No, I don't think it was the actual conditions of the camp, but it's... Now, for one thing, for instance, now there was... Of course, that might have been happened with... There's lots of black troops that went over there. And then one division that I was in, and there's a lot of boys that... They set them up, not before they were properly trained, but some of them that had never been on the rifle range. And take some of these kids out of the city, they didn't know hardly nothing about a rifle. I was from the country, and I always had a rifle in my hand ever since I was a little kid. But there's lots of fellas that didn't know nothing about handling guns, such as that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And further down to my experience, of course, you take about, I'd say, 75 or 80 % of the black troops that went over, they were non -combat. And of course, they had all the... I like the division I was in, the 92nd. Now, the engineers, our engineers were not combat engineers. And they were...most of them were stationed at Brest, France. And what they did, they built a camp there. They built a camp there, and then they unloaded the supplies. That was their job, unloading supplies. It takes lots of supplies to support an army, and that's the big port. And they stayed there, and that's what they did. Well, I don't know what you call it, stevedores or what, but that's their job, to unload the ships. And then they built barracks there. I came in when I came back when I came home. But when I went there, they were just building barracks in. And we stayed, but two days, 24 hours there, I asked them if the little pup got out in the mud, there was no place for us to stay, because there was no barracks. But this black engineering company was building barracks, seemed like other people's occupying them, just especially when we got in there. We didn't get any. We didn't have no barracks. Florida was in there, they didn't have no barracks for that quarter. And that's one thing, but outside of, they had, of course, as long as you're not, you either belong to combat or non -combat, and if you're non -combat, well, you just have to do anything.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I know they built the, they had lots of black chain in the ground. The GRS, the Gray Registration Service, and those fellows in that, why they, their job was to pick up these, pick up the dead, bury the dead. And they built, they built a cemetery, I know they built a cemetery out in Romaine, France. They were, there's lots of blacks out there, troops. They was, they was attached to an engineering outfit, but there wasn't a black engineer. White, White Engineering Outfitters was the head of it, and a lot of these, a lot of these black troops was, was assigned to this, this engineering troop. That is, that's why they got their mail, and that's why they got their money through this engineering company they were assigned to. And they had, this engineering company was building, building coffins, rough coffins, you know, to bury the dead in. And there was a German prison camp there also, in Garden Fritters, and I was the last, the last, after I come out of the Oregon Woodline, and I was assigned to the GRS, but I didn't, my job was to go back over the, over the ground, to the battlefield at the 92nd Division that the folks crossed over, and a few others, and, and I was in the grave registration service, and it gave you so much, so many numbers, and names, before fellows were supposed to guide in that section, or take that section, and go over, go over that, and find every little cross, everybody was killed there, had a, was, was buried, had a little cross at his grave, and one of his dog tags on it, you know. And we, our job was to get the, this dog tag number, name, and turn it, turn it into the headquarters at the grave registration service, and, oh, this, this, we had different districts to go on, go in, to cover, so much to cover, you know, and we were supposed to get their name, and now, so everybody that was buried in this, in this district, and it turns into the, to the headquarters, and then, of course, then the headquarters, they were assigned, when the fellows went out to pick up these bodies, they'd give them numbers in certain districts, if they had, that we'd give to them, you know, when they'd go out, they had certain ones in that district to pick up those bodies, and that's the way they, that's the way they kept track of the dead, and, and they got them buried. And that wasn't a very pleasant job, of course, they wasn't, they wasn't, the fellows that joined it wasn't, they wasn't normal blacks, but they, most of them were blacks, most of the blacks did the, dug the, dug the grave, I know they dug grave long trenches, was like a quarter of a mile long, or maybe not that long, and they, that was all hid by hand, and that was all hid with the black troops, those they were, they were what they call a pioneer, a pioneer infantry, or something like that, of course, they are, they are, they're not a, they're not a, they're not a combat infantry, they're just either work or work, but they had rifles, they'd either work or fight, shoot to fight if you got cornered, but that wasn't, they didn't, they weren't supposed to call up on the front lines, that's kind of what I'm saying, but.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So what it amounted to was that, was that black troops tended to get the more menial work to do, black troops tended to get the more menial work to do, and didn't have as much of a chance to get in the fighting.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's right, they were, most of the black troops that went over in World War I, most of them was, this fellow says most of them were non -combat, of course, not work troops, there's a pioneer infantry there, or engineers, or non -combat engineers is what most of them were, but there's one, 366, if you can get the outfit, in the 91st Division, 96th Division, they were, they were up, we were up in, in, and then they, up twice, they were up in, in the Oregon woods, up in the Vosges Mountains in France, the first time, then they came out, in long, towards the last of the war, that's when I went in, they went in, back in Oregon woods, again, they was in twice, and they were in the 16th Street, that's when I was in, we were in, or, the Oregon, and I don't know, I don't know if, of any of them, if there's any other black troops on any other front except the Oregon or not, well, you see, we didn't have any engineers, and we were backed up by, we were backed up by French, French engineers, you see, I didn't mean engineers, but, but, French, artillery, we didn't have no, we didn't have no artillery of our own, we, we were backed up by French artillery when I was in, I guess the rest of them went in the first time, well, I know they wasn't, the first time they went in, they wasn't, I know they was backed up by American artillery, again, because, the 29th, 29th artillery, because Harry Truman was the first Luke Kennedy in the that's the, that's the, that's the first time that I was up in the water mountains, I wasn't in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the American artillery better?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know whether it was any better, I don't know whether it was any better, I never, I never, I never heard anything whether it was any better or not. Well, it's stunning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The pup tents in the, on the mud, was that, would you say that was an example of a, of how black troops were treated?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, that's, well, that's just one of the, I figured it was just one of, that's just one of those things, you know, when we landed on Brest, well, as I said, these, there was no barracks there, and what there was was occupied, and they was building them, they was building the barracks there then, you know, for two foot weeks, and we got there, there wasn't room for all of us, and some of us, they had, I don't know why, company, I don't know what's the plus, we were, we just stepped out in the mud there, and I don't know how many the rest of them were, and the second night we got there, we moved into the barracks, it wasn't finished, and we carried in a roof, so we carried in boards and put them down over the mud, and just getting ready to go to bed, and then we got orders to move. That's right, and you told me about that. Yeah, yeah. Well, you said to me that, that all the officers were white in the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were these guys like as far as the way that they dealt with their troops? I mean, were they, were they really good officers?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, well, I would say, I would say that I don't think, I think the treatment from their officers, I can't say only from my outfit, and what company I was in, like, they got a lot, they were very good, I can see much, of course, they were lots, all the officers, they, in World War I, they followed the book better, mostly, than they did, you know, later on, the officer was an officer, and that's it, but in, I don't know, I don't know if the boys was in World War I, but then they, the officers, in a way, was a little closer to the troops, you know, in a way, they, of course, what I mean, in this way, now, you take a, you know, it don't matter where you met an officer, you know, you better salute him, or you'll get called down about it, but, and then, in World War II, why they tell me there wasn't so much that way, they just tell the officers, they didn't want you to care about your salute, and all that, and that is off of the post, or something like that, but that, there, but in World War I, why that is, there's more of a trick about that, than all the rules, I think, but outside of that, I think that if you did your job, and if you sold your way, I think, I think about all, of course, I can't say only from where I was, I don't know what some of the rest of them, they had lots of stuff than I did, but, in fact, I didn't have it too rough, myself, because I was in so many different places at the time, and I got the promotion pretty fast, the sergeant, and so on.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't, I can't, I have, my own, personally, I have, I have got no complaints about the what I found out when I was living there, if you, if you, if you soldier, are you alright?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, sir, if you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I would feel better about that if, if, if there had been black commissioned officers at the time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, in fact, they should have, they, I understand now that I don't know, I don't know just for sure, I heard that after the, after the first one, after the first encounter, and then when they came back, and went back into Oregon, and I heard that they had a lot of, you see, there was a lot of, in this division, there's a lot of, there's, it's only, I guess it's not the only division over there that I had heard of that had regulars in it, some of the blacks from the old 24th and 25th centuries, some older, older men, but I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From before the war, you mean?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, from before, and then they went over with this 92nd division, but I didn't, I didn't encounter any of them. The only black officers I saw over there were doctors, or dentists, or outside of sergeants, and such as that, but, I mean, police officers, but I never, you know, I imagine, and I imagine though, probably were, some, if I'd have hung around, dressed long enough, I'd probably have seen some of them down there with this engineer outfit, I imagine there may have been some there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember whether the troops did much singing? Whether what? Whether the troops did much singing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because I've read that there was a - Oh yes, yes, yes, we were, when we went up the first time, we had a, we had a, I don't know how long they'd be hiked, when I was going up to the front line, and they did, they'd march a while, and then they'd sing a while, they'd just, they did lots of singing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You'd take time, you know, you'd get to put your leg where you had a track on, and that, and somebody would start singing, and we'd just walk along and sing, and then sing a while, and they'd go up and all the time, we'd march.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the songs, were the songs, were they like popular songs, or were they war songs, or songs that people made up?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: War songs, most of them were war songs, uh huh. Most of them were war songs, I got a songbook here somewhere, I don't know where it is, but I got it, most of them would just come up during the war, what's current in the war, like, that's what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any difference in benefits for white and black troops when they got back home after the war was over?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Not that I know of, no.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The only thing I did hear though, now, was just, where I heard, the worst thing I heard about the war, is, of course, is discrimination of blacks and whites, but when they, I heard that now, after some of the, some of the, well, now my dad was, he, my mother was from Mississippi, and they went back down there, it was in 19, 1919, I believe, the first year I was home, no wonder it was the second year. Anyway, and, and my dad said, they told him that down in the current Mississippi, that's where my mother folks, my mother was born, and she said, when the black boys come from the army, and when they got off the train, they made them get all of their uniforms. And my dad told me that's what they told him down, happened to them down in Mississippi. And the worst thing, the worst thing that I heard, I remember reading, and heard it when I got home, is that many of the boys that were sick or disabled, was getting hospitalization.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There was no place for them to go to be cared for. They wouldn't take them, they were segregated, you know, they wouldn't take them in the regular hospitals, and there was no veterans hospital. I heard that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean when they got back to the United States?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, when they got back to the United States, yeah. And I was, I was in Virginia, first in 60, 60, I had a brother's teaching down there, in Petersburg, Virginia, just about 35 miles from Richmond, and I had had a stroke here when I retired, and I was supposed to have a checkup every so often, so I was down there, and I come time for me to check up, and so I told my brother, I wanted him to take me up to Richmond, that was a big veteran's hospital up there. He said, you don't have to go up there, he said I can get you through and go through the hospital right here on the campus, I can give you a checkup, I'll take you in, they'll take you in there. Well, I told him no, I said, I want to go to this hospital, I just want to go in there and see for myself if the blacks here, they're segregated and treated any different to anybody else, I want to know how they treat me to go in there, and just to see how severe the segregation is in the veteran's hospital up there. I've heard so much about it right after war, and soon I went up there, and I never was treated any better in my life than I was in that hospital up there, I was only up there just to date overnight, about two days, about two days, about all I was there just for checkups was all I went in for, and I was never treated any better in any veteran's hospital, and I've been in the four, in Boise, and I've been in Oakland, California, and I've been in Spokane about four times, and I've never been treated any better than I was treated down there at McGuire Hospital.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say they had to take the uniforms off, you mean before they could get off the train?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, when they got off, they made them take them off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they got off?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they didn't want them parading around in a government uniform. They didn't want them parading around in a government uniform.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what they call the race riots that took place after the war was over in some of the big cities?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It was right around 1919 and 1920, I think, there were some riots that was largely the same.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Right after the war. No, I really don't remember any. Well, I remember all this race trouble we had in the 60s and that, but right after the war, I really don't. The only thing I remember is the trouble with all the World War I soldiers when they went to march on Washington, D .C. on this bonus business, and they turned it, and they drove them out of town and all of that stuff. That's a long time, but in the race riots right after the war, I really don't remember Well, I'm not sure if it was, it might have been a few years, it might have been right at the beginning of the 20s, but I know it was, anyway, I've read about it. I really don't remember, as I say, I don't recall any. It's only in the early 60s, along through that when it's happened so much. But anything before that, I really don't recall. I won't say they didn't have, but. The guy that was the first black heavyweight champion, that was Jack Johnson. Jack Johnson, uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did he come in to become champion? Were you just an old boy then?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I was just a little boy then. I remember, but I was just a little boy then. Why I tell you, why I remember so well, we had the, we had the 4th of July celebration here in Moscow, and I was, dad had this kid that we was in town with, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they would say, did he become champion on the 4th of July or right around that time? I don't know, I know he had a big fight right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, well I remember that, but anyway, the guys had up a concession down here on on Third Street there, of course the bell tower is there now, but it's kind of what, right there where the city hall is now on that block, that big bell tower there. And they had a concession up there, and they had a, they had a big canvas stretched up and a hole in it, and the black guy had his head through there, and he was holding him, keep Jack Johnson, just throwing balls at him. And I remember that, that's when he became, that's when Jack Johnson became champion, when he won the championship, and I don't remember whether he was on the 4th of July or around that, but I know I remember well, this guy, this canvas he had up there and that hole, and the black guy had his head in there, and he had so many balls, he was so for so much, and he was quite a, that's what I thought this haulin', he was Jack Johnson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you were, and your brothers were quite a hand with the gloves, you told me, you liked that, you liked to spar around in boxing, did you pay attention to Jack Johnson's career?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, and we didn't get the idea from Jack Johnson. Well I wouldn't imagine you did, but I just mean that. No, well I'll tell you why this glove business came in, now, it was the one Christmas, there's a fellow that, he's a black man, he's been around in parts of the country, he's just a laborer, he had a little place up at Deer Park, out of Spokane, so he came down one Christmas, and he brought us a pair of boxing gloves, give to us kids, you know, there's a present for all four of us boys and all, and that's where the boxing started in our family, and the first thing that Dad told us after we got those gloves, that he says I don't care how much your kids box among yourselves, as long as you don't get mad, and another thing, if you, I don't want you going out, and any time I hear you going out picking on somebody else, some other kid to kind of show your skill, either one of those things, he says I hear lots of the boxing gloves go in the stove, and my dad, when he gave an order, he wasn't just whistling Dixie a few minutes, and that's where the boxing started in the family, we had boxing gloves on, then we got boxing gloves on the back porch, and boxing gloves on out in the barn, and we had a saying among us, we got kids to do one, say something, and they'll want to dispute him about something, don't agree with him, and we got caught up in something like that, so I wasn't angry, but we just sat through it, we wanted to say something, say no you were wrong, and he says ok go get the gloves, that's the way we settled our argument, we settled our argument with the gloves, there was no hard feelings there, don't you think that, that was just the way we did it, and so we got pretty good, and we liked it, and there was a good support, and we did it, and every Sunday, we'd get a barnyarder's for the kids up there, and we'd go boxing, and all that fun, and nobody got hurt in the life, and we'd be a family, so that's why boxing got started in the settled boy's family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This man that gave you the gloves, was he much of a friend of the family?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah he had worked for my dad there, one or two seasons, we knew him.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he came down to Christmas, and he bought us all a present, and that's the best he brought us boys, was those boxing gloves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he got connected up with your dad?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I don't know, he was a Spanish American war veteran, for one thing, and I think he was down in this country looking for work I think, he was the same fella, worked for my wife's folks down at Pomeroy in early days, he was a young man. And I think it was through him, I'm not sure, we were through this fella, that we heard about this other black family down in Pomeroy, and that was my wife's folks. I don't know, I won't say for sure for this, but I don't think he, he knew them, but I was a kid, the young men up here that worked for the farm labor, they would go down in the lower country around the Pomeroy country and Walla Walla country every spring in January because work started quicker down there, you know, ended up around here, and I think that's why we were sort of the some fellas from here going down working down in that country. That's why we heard from Lynn, Lynn this fella worked down there, he worked down there, he's an old Wyoming man.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You know, and you talked about going to the church up in Spokane, and Mr.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Wiggins,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Reverend Church was, and I didn't know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he went to the Methodist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a black congregation?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very big?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it wasn't, well, it was a pretty good size, pretty good size church for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he in the house?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, he's in there. I don't, I wouldn't say what the size, what the congregation was, but it is, what the number, but it is a pretty good size church. Of course it wasn't, I don't think it's as big as the one they have up there now, or, you know, they're being in a big city, but it is, it was a pretty fair size to the little church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get to meet the daughter of the Reverend?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You said that you were going with her. Well, I tell you, I happened to do that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Her sister had a, her sister was going to school over here at Pullman, and as a black boy here going to the University of Idaho, he run a, he had a little, one share barbershop up there on the campus, up there, of course, why they had all the oil there, just to go up to the campus there. He had a barbershop there, kind of working his way through school, and then he, he got acquainted with his girl's sister over in Pullman, and so he bought her over here and bought her out to our house for dinner one time, so that's why I got a crime for her sister. That's how I happened to, to meet her sister is through her. So when I come to Spokane, I come out to see them, so I went out to meet her folks, and that's how I got started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The people that went to the church there in Spokane, the Methodist Church, did, did they, were they more or less a community of their own, or did they come from all over Spokane?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: All over Spokane, all over Spokane, there, they, see there was a Baptist church and a Baptist church nearby, and I believe that this girl, her, her dad, he wasn't, he wasn't either, he was a minister himself, but he wasn't, I believe he was a congregation minister, something like that, but he didn't have no church there in Spokane. Yeah, well, they attended the Baptist, the Baptist, but the Baptist, the Baptist Church was large, quite a bit larger than the Baptist in Spokane. There's more Baptist than the one that I met at the church. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you become acquainted with many, with many people up there? Huh? Did you become acquainted with many, with many other people up there in Spokane?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I, no I didn't, you know, that, yeah, you know, when I, I never had very much association with, with black people outside of my family, very, very little until after I went in the army, because, now you take my folks had a few black friends in Spokane, came down and visited, and they went up there and visited.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was in Spokane quite a little bit, now you see after I met this girl, I used to go up there, go up there on Sundays, some Sundays, go out there and of course we'd go out together and, and I only, I only, I went to, and then after the war, well just before the war, just before the war, my wife's brother, she had two brothers, they lived up to the Oak Hill Inn, and they came up there and then they had a fellow, another black boy working for them, they came up there and this fellow's working for him, he had an aunt up there, and this aunt of his gave a party for the, for the boys out of town, and of course she didn't know I was in town, and so I didn't get no invite, but, but through this girl, I was going with, she asked her to come over, we'd take her on the books, you know, so that's where I went, and she called up and told her, she said she told her to bring me, she told her to bring me on out, so we went out there, and that's the only party I was ever to of the black, young black people up there, and that's all that happened, that's the one of the things, of course. When I was in service, I met quite a few of the, quite a few of the boys from Spokane at Camp Lewis, but we all went there, all of us from Spokane, and then we went to Camp Lewis there, and then we didn't get separated until we went to Kansas City, and then we got separated, and then I didn't, and there wasn't any, there wasn't any Spokane boys in, in my outfit, in Iowa.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, your, your folks, your folks' friends that they, that they had from up there, were they friends of theirs, and long standing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, they just, they just met them, this was just like, through the church and stuff, not too much through the church, but just, well there was a, I guess, how they got started, how they got acquainted with Spokane up, the black people from Spokane up there, it's mostly true that, that people that, that worked up on here, the blacks that worked here on the campus from Spokane, come down here and work, and through them, I got it, and then, and then my sister, she, she was in Spokane for, she was, she went to Spokane quite a bit, and through her, there was a lot of animals, we have them, and they used to visit back and forth, and what not.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But we never, we never, our family, we never did have too much, very, a lot of association with our people. Well, you know, in those days it was, and that is, that is really before automobile time, and transportation is pretty slow.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then the first, then, when I came back, when I came back from the Army, the year I was the first black graduate of the University of Idaho, her son entered, entered school down here, she was from Spokane. Well, I guess it's probably under some of these other, other I told you, under some of these other conversations, we landed in Moscow, we, we wasn't coming to Moscow, Idaho, until we landed here, we landed here, we was going to Moscow, Washington, we landed here, and we got here, there was two businesses, two businesses, black businesses in Moscow, one family ran a restaurant, and another family ran, another fellow had a barber shop, now I don't remember whether it was all in the same place, same building or not, but I know that the fellow by the name of McCree, they ran a restaurant, and there was quite a hangout for the college students, and then the fellow by the name of Christman, he had a barber shop downtown, and his daughter was the first black graduate from the University of Idaho, and she had just about started, when they started in Idaho too, but along about that time, pretty early, and she was the first, I don't know whether she was the first graduation class, or the second one, somewhere along there, it wasn't pretty soon after she was started, and he went to, they lived in Spokane, their home was in Spokane, they went to Spokane, and this girl married a fellow from, I don't know, somewhere back east, what was it, Chicago, what, he was running on the railroad, and he bought, that's when the gold was discovered up north in the northern parts of the state here, there was lots of mines, and he bought, he had, he bought a bunch of shares in some mine up there, you know, and they eventually became pretty well off, paid off on him, you know, and then, anyway, anyways, when her son, her son is an old graduate, got to high school right before college, she wanted him to go down to Tides, she lived down here in Idaho, and he went the first semester, and then he went home, and come home, come back for the second semester, got the flu,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and right after the war, right after the war, he passed away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you knew, you knew Chrisman's daughter a little?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I didn't know Chrisman's daughter, I didn't know the daughter, I met this kid's mother after he passed away, she came down, and we met her then, but I didn't know who Chrisman's daughter was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well then there was the family around Tents at the Plummer that you mentioned. Yeah, Kings, Kings. And in fact, I guess there's still, there's still, there's still some water material there, yeah, there's plenty of water material there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know them at all?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I met the King boy when I was at Camp Lewis, that's when I met him, that's when I met him, the King boy in Camp Lewis, and we were friends until we got separated, and then we got together afterwards. Now here's, now here's one of the, here's a fellow, and he was in a, he was in a different outfit than I had, and I think he had a little rougher time than I did, but I don't know how badly.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, that family is still like, kinda like ours. He there was a, there's a, two and a one, three, I think he had six, I think there were six children, and three boys,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and only seven kids, I think there's three girls and four boys.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, see the old, the youngest girl, she's still alive, and the oldest boy, and the next oldest boy, there's two of the boys left, and the youngest girl is well -lamped up there now. Well, the girl isn't there, she's in a, she's in a kind of an old folks' home, rest home up in Spokane, and the boys, the next oldest, the oldest boy, his wife are there, and he was in, he was in here, he was out singing up here this fall, this summer rather, and he was in a, he went to, just got out of his hospital in Spokane, and was going back, and they brought his wife down here, and she's out here in one of these rest homes here, she was not very good.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's a good boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say to you about what happened in his experience in the war that makes you think he had a rough time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he didn't have too good a time, he was in, he was in a pioneer entry out, whether it was a workout business, he got into that. So, anyway, anyway, he helped, he helped, he helped build this cemetery out there, at the domain of France, he was one of the fellows that dug the graves, helped dig the graves out there. I mean, he worked, he worked just like the common labor would have anything else, he didn't know him, but he was, he put in a lot of his time with a thick and a shovel, that's what I meant to, and I didn't. My hardest work was packing and hiking, packing a heavy pack, that was my hardest.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Anyway, so he don't think much of his army experience.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But as far as any mistreatment from the officers, I don't know, I don't think I ever heard him, I never heard him say anything about that like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That king, is that spelled K -A -N -E or K -I -N -G?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: King, yeah, K -I -N -G, uh -huh.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When we talked about socializing, when you were growing up, and the dances, that sort of thing, you said that you never danced, only your sister, only your sister, we had one sister that did. And I know like with the Wells, some of the stories that people tell about the Wells family, out in Erie, was that that was a mark of how well they were accepted in it, you know, it didn't matter at all, because they all danced with the, it didn't matter, the dances. But in your family, most of the kids didn't dance. We didn't take no dancing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I tell you, I think that, well, I know too, the Young, some of the Young, the Wells, the Young did, maybe they did, I don't know, I won't say for sure, but I think that most of the dancing they did, the Wells, most of it just is these house party dancing, I won't say for sure, but I know the, one of the grandsons, I know that he used to go to dances up there in the Erie, and so on. But with the older ones, I think that pioneer days, the older days, I think they just used to, you know, give these house parties, such as that, and I'd say, if you dance, I won't, I could be mistaken on that, because I didn't know. No, that's probably true, that that was in pioneer times. Yeah, I don't think so. I think that's the most they did. Get together and have a party and then dance, somebody could play turkey and straw in a while. Right.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's what I kind of think more than anything else.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I still, I wouldn't say that's true, but I think that's most of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in the case of your family, though, how come that your sister did and the rest of you did?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: as I told you, she was, she went to Spokane more than the rest of them. She got to see young people up there, and she danced up there, and then it was just like down here, with those kids and young people, you know, neighborhood parties, something like that, and somebody, sometimes some of them danced, and that's where she started, that's where she got her start, to dance, but I never cared for dancing, I never did dance, and I had one brother that, basically he danced a little bit, but dancing didn't speak to the voice for some reason or other.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But you guys would go to the party, we'd go to a party or neighborhood, get together anyway, but you just wouldn't dance. Oh, we didn't dance, just, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Like my wife and I, we were married down on the country there, and we used to have parties there, and we'd play cards a while, and then we'd dance a while, but I never did dance. But I always enjoyed anybody that was a good dancer, I liked to dance that way, but I just got, I got, I was one of those guys that got two left feet. Most of the family, you know, that's something that's very funny about the Seattle family, is the very few black families I know of, that there wasn't some of them, some of them in that group was, was inclined, musical inclined, or, or singing, could sing. Now you take yourself, kids, oh, my sister, she played the, she played the, she played the organ a little, piano a little bit, and. But us boys, we never, there was none of us to sing. I thought about it after I sold my wife outside and all, and most kids could have been the talented thing, and got up a quartet, settled by the quartet, like she was really good at it, but it just wasn't in it. And, well, I guess we kind of got a, we kind of got a natural, always, but. My dad, folks, some of them were too new, but my mother, my grandmother, that he was a, he was a great banjo picker.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And after he got married,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: the first child was born, he hung that old banjo up on the wall, and hung there till the day, until dad, dad went down there in 1919 and tore the old, tore the old house down, built for Grandma and London. No, you never, I remember when we was coming out here, we went up through there, coming right west where we stopped at Grandma's, and we tried to get Grandpa to play that, play for us, but he never, he wouldn't do it, he never took that thing down either. Religion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh? He got religion?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, oh yeah, no, he used to play for dances, you know, and go out and dance and drink, and he used to sing and all that, but when he got married, he cut her all out, and hung up his banjo and that was it, he got religion.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, in other words, when the, the fact that you didn't dance around here, you could have if you wanted to.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yes, oh yes, I had, shoot, I had a chance to go to the dance block, I think when I went to high school, I was more attracted to dance, I had lots of chances to dance, I didn't want them to dance, but I just, just didn't. What are these parties that you had after you got married, would they just be, just uh, Social parties, we'd play the pinocle, play cards, you know, and have lunch and dance. Usually somebody had, we had one or two guys in the neighborhood that, and violin, play violin, and they'd dance, and they'd dance, and play cards, and the fact of the matter is some of them danced, and some of them, after all, they were not danced, and some of them, they didn't want to dance or play cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be at a person's house or at the school?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, oh yeah, just at the first house, no, just their house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many couples would get together?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, oh gosh, we used to have, the party used to come around, there was 12, 15.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Couples?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh no, not a couple people, uh huh. Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You know, there's an old farmer friend of mine, and I don't know if you know of Gilders at all, the Gilder family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gilder?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Gilder, yeah. I haven't heard of Gilder, but I haven't. He grew up around Harvard. I never Gilder'd, yeah. He lives, Glenn Gilder is his name, he lives near Troy now. Anyway, he used to be my neighbor out there, and I lived out there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I know the name, but I don't really know the people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he kind of got me talking to people in the first place. It was partly his idea, mainly his idea, I should. But anyway, he taught me how to play pinocle not too long ago, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There were four couples there, including us, there were eight people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, gosh, it seemed like we played pinocle halfway through the night. Yeah, yeah, yeah, we played, we used to play crowd parties and the grand. I know we had, you know, Stella and I had one party, we had a big house. We had a party that night, and I don't know how many, we had a house full, and a couple big snowstorms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A couple big what, snowstorms?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: A couple of warm and the snow was blowing, and four of them, two couples stayed all night. And went out the next morning. The postmaster's dad and his wife, there was one couple that stayed there, and then this fellow runs a travel service down near the travel service. His mother -in -law and father -in -law, they stayed, those four stayed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's the postmaster?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Buchanan, Howard Buchanan. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, what a pinocle get together, it could go for, it was a big, it could be a long, up there. And usually people had eats. Oh, yeah, well, that was one of the main things, just eat. Yeah, we always had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people talk at first, would they socialize first and then play pinocle after they'd been there for a while?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, we usually played pinocle to start with, and then we had to serve some kind of lunch, you know. And then after lunch, that's when somebody get off the old violin, and someone wanted to dance, and we'd dance and others play pinocle, and someone do a little of both. Of course, we cut out on, we didn't have so many playing pinocle, but that time, sometimes there wouldn't be enough playing, enough for a table. But that's the way they did it. They always had dance and then, and dance playing pinocle. They kind of had their choice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be mostly in the winter, would folks do it in the summertime?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah, no, that's in the wintertime. No, not in the summertime. We had no parties in the summertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Too busy, huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Too busy, no, but it's the wintertime. That is the wintertime. That's in the wintertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would they be, say, in the winter?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, we first married, there's one every week. Thought it'd be at our house this week, and it's one of the rest of next week, kind of go around, and back and forth, and so, till spring work, and till the snow went off, so we couldn't go on the sled, and something like that, and then they'd break up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty much just right within the neighborhood?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it was in the neighborhood, yeah, neighborhood, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The near neighbors?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, it took in quite a bit of territory. So, I come up and says, well, I'll say no, I wouldn't say no, because we lived, I guess, our place, we was just about the end of the neighborhood on the east, then I think they went west about five or six miles, probably about a ten -mile square, something like that, probably not that big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the other couples young couples like yourselves, or were they other?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, they were some older, some older. There was, let's see now, there was Stella and I, and the old kid from Martha, Ben Johan, and his wife.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, there was about, there was four couples, well, like Stella and I, this young married people, and the rest of them were older people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Some of them were quite a bit older. Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'll tell you, it seemed to me, it seemed to me like really a lot of fun. Yeah, we had lots of fun. I wish I'd learned how to play years ago. And then, then in our neighborhood, of course, the school mom, she boarded in the neighborhood, and of course, she is.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Julie, she stayed there over the weekend, and she'd have her boyfriend, and he'd be there, and that would be another young couple. They followed them, they followed them pretty close. They didn't have something else special to do. It helped the winter to go past her. We always had something to look forward to in the next life. We were always, we were out about 10 to 12 miles from Moscow,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and 50 years ago, there wasn't so many automobiles either. Of course, we all had cars, you know, but in those days, we used to run a car in the shed in the wintertime and blocked it up and sit there, extremely, but now they drive them all the time. I know the first two years Estella and I were married, we didn't. Well, one winter, we used to order a car in Moscow here, and then one winter, we put it in the shed and blocked it up, and then after that, we started driving it all winter. We'd chain them up and drive them when we could, and when we couldn't, we'd pick a team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But we always had it ready to drive, and when we could drive, we'd do what?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Get the used to, you know, and winter would come when you'd jack your car up and put it on blocks and let it sit there, that's the thing, but now, no matter how much it is, they'd drive them all winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the main form of socializing at the time? Was that the main way people could get it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's the main way of socializing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the school? Did the school get much use for socializing too back then?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, of course, the schools, the country schools, they had quite a little bit, and I like Christmas time. They had a Christmas tree, you know, on a program and such as that, you know, and different holidays, the kids, they had a celebration, but they always had a big Christmas tree on Christmas time, and then I'd hold everybody wet, and I had a good time then. And every so often, sometimes they want to raise some money for something, they'd have a basket so true, you know, and such things as that. Kids had quite a bit of socializing among themselves. But say for, there weren't really dances in school. Oh no, no, no, no. That's our thing at the schoolhouse there. No, no, they didn't, no, there were no dancing schools. Some places I know they did use them for that, but I guess, it sounds to me like Pinocchio was by far the most popular card game too. Yeah, that's kind of old -timers game, where Pinocchio didn't come up cards. No, as long as I remember, as long as I ever went to country school, and as long as I lived in the country, I never remember the young people having to dance in the schoolhouse or anybody else. Now, that's where we lived. We lived what you call kind of the Linville country down in there, and there's an old store down there. There used to be a store there when I was a kid. Now, that was a great entertainment center down in there. They had big dances down there in that old store about every Saturday night, or every so often down there, and that was a big gathering place for young and old people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it still being used as a store?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, it was abandoned. It was abandoned. I see. The building was still there, and that's what they used it for, as a store. And the schoolhouse right next to us, when they abandoned that way, they made a kind of a community center out of it. They were good, but I don't think, I don't, they never had any dances down there, I don't think. Well, I tell you, at that time, all them buildings got so plentiful, they didn't dance out in the country. They went somewhere else. They went to Spokane or Houston or Genesee over there. That was too close to home. We stopped there and danced. The young people, they got out of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: All they used it for is, oh, just to have parties for different people and wedding anniversaries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Parties for, you say parties for people? Huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They had parties for people. Yeah, old corn, like wedding anniversaries or something like that. And, of course, they had those kind of women, they had a kind of schooling club, some kind of club, and they met there. But I never heard of them having to dance in there. They might have. That's how I left down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you guys really so busy during the summer farming that you didn't have time to socialize at all? Or would you, on a weekend or something like that, get together with another couple or something? Well, I'll tell you, isn't it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In our household, after the Army, after I got an open Army and we got an automobile,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: my brother next to me and my sister, they socialized quite a bit. They'd go to Spokane to parties and something like that, one thing and another quite a bit. But the rest of us boys, we didn't get in too much, and we never socialized too much with us. And the same way as this other black family up at the area, the Kings up there, well, we never socialized the same way. We got up there quite a little bit, but not very much.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The welders, I meant to say.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The welders.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we never did quite a bit. Well, the boys worked for us. I worked for them quite a bit. But both only settled with my sister, and rather they socialized with more than the rest of them. I worked for them up there one winter. I worked for them one winter. The first winter I was back in the Army, I worked in the woods. I worked for the old man. He was a gyppo up there in Logan. I worked there in the winter.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But they were a little faster set than the settlers.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, you know, what I really had in mind was, say, what you said, you said it was out to have pinochle parties in the summer. And I figure the reason you're saying that was because you were working hard. People didn't have time to sit around and spend half the night playing cards. But what I was wondering about is if people had time to socialize, even during the summer, but it was a different kind of getting together. If we'd get together after dinner or something like that with a neighbor, how that used to be.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, that socialized then was a little bit different sometimes. Well, I like, you know, Kelly and I, when we were younger, we always had some, oh, one or two family close friends, and we'd always get together on weekends and go way up the level of fishing trips and such like that, camp out, you know, on fishing fish and stuff like that. That's what those socializations were when we were on the farm. If we had any time to spare, we'd, us and another couple, maybe two, three couple would get together and go on a fishing trip or outing at that time. But of course, dancing and other parties, we never really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these close friends of yours the same as your pinochle partners?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, oh yes. I mean, they were the same people. Yeah, some of the same people. Some of the same people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I, when you, you sound like, from what you're saying, and this is something I've heard before, that when the cars came in, the people went out, and it sort of hurt or made less close the neighborhood.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I'm gonna tell you, before the cars came in,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: you knew your neighbor. But after cars came in, you don't hardly know your neighbor. There's not enough socializing, what I'm trying to tell you. There wasn't so much socializing among the neighborhood.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Until the socialize in the neighborhood, they get in the car and go to Spokane or go to Moscow or somewhere else to some movie or some other kind of entertainment. That's why you see. That's why they just, the cars just broke up the north, and that's the most social life in the country. You take that day, the kids, they don't hardly know their neighbors. They always go somewhere else. They want to go somewhere.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And also, that's out. Neighborhood dance, and that's all out. Neighborhood party, that's out. Also, the cars just broke out all the peace, and then those days, it was horseback. You go horseback.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it's too bad because it seems like for all the benefits of having a car, there's something that's real. Yes, it is. That's right. The car did it. They just broke it down. You take it from Del and I's first marriage, lived out there in the country. Now, we're neighbors, get together, and of course, those days, that's why we always butchered our own meat, hogs. Every farmer, he always butchered one or three or four hogs every year for his own meat. Well, maybe we'll go over here, and we'll go over, and this state go over one or two and help Gee and Del a butcher their pigs. You know, here comes a bunch of man, his wife and all, and he'll help us butcher. And then have a big dinner, and probably chew the fat a while, and then probably in a day or two or something, we'd go over to the other end and help him butcher, and they're slow and social. But now, we don't have no hogs. The farmers don't have no hogs anymore. They go to butcher. So the farm and social life is just between automobiles and a tractor, it just, there's no social life on the farm anymore, you might say.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When we talked about the Depression, we talked, you told me about the economics of it. I mean, why, how the, up into the Depression, even in the late 20s, how the tractor come in and the horse went out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, huh? But what about socially?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was thinking what life was like for people on the farm, just...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I tell you, when the horses, when the tractor came in and the horses went out, as I said a while ago, we talked about butchering hogs. The hogs left the ranch. The chickens left the ranch. And all they had was a tractor and probably a milk cow.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And that's about all there was to it, and they wanted to go somewhere. You turn off the tractor, go to town, get the old lady ready, get ready, and get in their car, go to town or somewhere else. Well, there's nothing to do. They didn't have no horses to take care of. They had no other livestock to take care of, so they had time on their hands. When they turned that tractor off, they didn't have to take it to the barn and feed it and curry it. No, sir, they'd accept that, oh, sir. I tell you what. You'd leave for a couple days and never notice the difference at the time. That's right, sure. So they'd go. Yes, sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But during the Depression, when money was scarce and you didn't want to spend it on the gas, that kind of thing, did people get closer again?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, that's as long as still in our time, and we were married. See, we were married during the Depression. That's the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year were you married?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We married in 1924. Uh -huh. And so that is why it is so easy to have these parties and get big crowds up because they couldn't, they felt if they had cars, they couldn't drive and wouldn't drive them in the wintertime, you know, and they all, that's when they had their time. And they snowed in. And sometimes, over there, we used to have, there'd be times that you'd get snowed in over there for two, three days' time until you get the road plot out. So they didn't have all this equipment then to keep the roads plot out. School buses wasn't running. And not only got to keep the roads plot out on how school was, the guys can drive the car all winter. But over there, where we lived, we had the country school and there wasn't no school buses running. So you'd hook up the team, take your kids to school, or you'd ride horseback or make your own trail. But the farm social life was gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did her folks come to live with you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, that was in, see, we married in 24. And I think this was 26 that they came. So they'd give up the farming and come and live with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did they remain alive very long?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, the LNC. See, that was in 26. And her dad died in 34, I believe.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And her mother passed away in 40.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was just thinking about that. It wasn't too long ago. I was thinking that, you know, I thought of my folks if they wanted to come and live with me. Well, I think it would be maybe easier now that I've been married for quite a few years. And I've been married just a couple.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was there being there, did that make it at all harder for you guys?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it made it harder on us. Of course, they didn't live right in the same house with us. I had another policeman and they lived in the house there. Not until, we had them, they lived in the house with us one way or another. And then after her father died, then her mother lived with us until she passed away. But the both of them, they didn't, it was the only, they lived in the same house with us one way or another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you help them get by?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you kind of support them?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we helped them. We helped them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That was quite a worry of mine. I don't wear it on the bottom of my wife. Because after her father died, then her mother, she kind of, like a lot of old people, she got kind of feeble -minded, you know. And she couldn't, she wasn't satisfied with us. After he left, she always said she wanted to go home, she said. And she figured she just visited with us, and she said she thought she wanted to go home. And then she'd run off and, my wife, she played pretty tough on my wife that for a while. That a part of her life.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: What about your folks and when they were getting older, did they stay at that place that they...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they lived out, they, they lived out on the farm out there, and so they got good old. And then my mother died, and then my father, he stayed out there until the house that he was living in burned down. And then he went down to Portland and lived with my sister. I guess he was down there about three or four years before he passed away.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There's something I wanted to ask you about. You told me about your brother who taught at college in Virginia. But I don't really know much about what the life stories are of your other brothers and sisters.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, my younger, my youngest brother, he died at the age of 21, so he didn't, he was still, he was still at home. He worked with my dad, my dad was, he ran a hay bale, pretty impressive. He worked at home, and he did a lot of farm work. He didn't go to school after he got to high school, he didn't go any further than that. And then my other brother, Jess, he, he just got, and right after I got married, he, well, my next brother, he got married,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and they didn't, he was married only two or three years, and he separated, and they separated.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then he went to teaming, got, he retired, he did teamwork around town here in Overton, he did Overton mostly, and he did teamwork over there, and then he worked Overton there quite a little while.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then he died Overton. He died a pretty young man, he died for seven years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was Jess, that was Jess?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then your youngest brother?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Was Booker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your older, the next brother?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: John, John.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was the one who went to Virginia?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He was the one, he was the one that, he was one of the saddle boys that went on and got his education, and he was the only one on the side of the floor that finished college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Jess passed his whole life in the area here, he stayed?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, he was here, but he, he never, Jess never finished high school, he went to, he just never got in his mother in the 8th grade, he was in 8th grade many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was about par for the time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, about the time, so he didn't go to school. And my sister didn't go to high school, but she lived out in the country there, when she left, and she wanted to go, and she wanted to go to Spokane Business College, and they couldn't send in, the folks couldn't afford to send up there, and she wanted to go up, she wanted to go to Spokane and get a job, and work with, of course, and folks said they wanted to go for that. So we went up there by ourselves to work, and they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't want to do that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She was a country kid, you know, of course she knew quite a few people in Spokane at that time, but they didn't like that idea, and she took a correspondence, took a correspondence course, and she got married pretty, got her job, and she was, back then, and she got her job at the university up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where, here in town?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, here in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was she doing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She was a maid up there at the Lassen Ball Hall, the girls hall, and she better contented.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Worked out here too, a few years, then got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did she meet her husband? Where was he from? Was he local?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I believe he was, I believe he was, I believe that he was originally from Kansas City, he was just a, he was, oh you might say he was just a transient.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She had two sons, and they finally separated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She and her husband? Huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She and her husband. Yeah, she and her husband, they separated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she in Moscow all this time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, she didn't live in Moscow all this time. No, she didn't live in Moscow at all after she married. She lived in Seattle a while.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then after, I guess after they lived in Seattle, that's why they separated in Seattle, and she came to, she came to Portland,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and the folks, they had two boys, and the folks took, they took the kids, and she worked there, she worked there, she worked in Portland for years,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and then she married a guy that was a railway mail clerk,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and then she did pretty good then, and she took the kids, and they built a home, and she took the boys, and so her and the boys are still down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's her name?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Uh, Reed. Oh, I mean her first name. Cleola. Cleola, huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And her sons are still living in Portland?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they're there, one of them, well, one of them lives in Tacoma, and the other one's in Portland. One of them ran a gas station down there for years, he was, uh, he was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she's still there then? Is she older than you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, she's older than I am. She's the oldest. Yeah, we're the only two settled kids left, two oldest kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, did she work very much when she was in Portland?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she worked, she worked quite a little bit until, until she got married, and after her husband died, she had, well, her health was too good, she had lots of chances to work, but she didn't know where we ever worked after that, but she worked some, but not very much. She's still, the last time I'm home, she had lots of good chances, lots of good job offers, but she just can't, isn't able, isn't able to take them, she doesn't know if they're very good anymore. Mm -hmm. Getting pretty well up in age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Jess ever, ever get married?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he never did get married. Uh -uh, he got married. But your brother, John, he - Yeah, he got married, he has one son now in Richmond, Virginia, he's a veteran there, down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he meet his wife back there? Huh? Did he meet his wife back there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, oh yes, yes, he met his wife, first year he was doing, down there to teach, he met her, she was teaching now, and we were both teaching at Virginia State.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you grew up, did, was the family really scattered, or did they get together, did they try to get together at all back here, in the folks' place?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we, after, our family kind of got scattered, after John left, and Booker and I and my sister left, but they, our family were, well we got,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: after John went east, back south, the whole family never got together very much after that, and then my mother died, and broke up pretty fast after John left home. And he wasn't gone too many years, and then my mother passed away.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: John's the one that you said was a pretty gutsy guy, young, as a young man. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He was a, I don't know, how was it that, what was it about him that, I don't know that you ever really explained to me why he was, what he was like, but I thought he was the sort of guy that would fight, or, he didn't have any fear of anything. No, he was a, he was one of the saddle boys, and he was a good guy, but, it cost him, he would fight.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But he never did, he wasn't so much a, he never had any trouble here, going to school, when he first went back down, went to Virginia, went to teaching down there, he had a little trouble, and he would give a different professor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A different what?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Different professor, he kind of got in, he kind of got, he kind of, as he said, he kind of got in a line, got out of line, as he called it, he said he guessed the color, and he got in some other professor's territory.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Some other young professor figured,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: as he said, as he called it, as he had been there, and they kind of figured they had a kind of a,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ring on the other young women's professor's, and they had some teachers up there, they kind of, and then he went down there, and of course he kind of,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: you know, you wouldn't get a date with one of them other women, because he's always stepping on some other professor's toe, you know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You didn't like having a new boy on the block. That's right, that's right, boy, and of course he is from the north, and I tell you, you take the northerner, go down south, then, I don't care whether he's black or white, black and white, either one of them didn't care too much for him, and so they called his hand on him, and soon as they got kicked off of him, he was trapped. He didn't back up.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I wonder if being a northerner too, he had, and growing up around here, you know, he might have had less of a notion of the, of where, you know, of like knowing his place or something like that, I mean, he just might have been much more independent than, I don't know, I'm guessing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know how he played, but he just, he just felt like it, and just like anybody else was, I feel the same way. I went to down there and went to see some cool shows, and I don't know if I saw a young lady, a teacher, or was it, a young lady, I think, looking for a date, I would ask her, and if she accepted, why, I'd take her off. You bet. So that's what he did, but,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: there was some other guy that had been dating, you know, he didn't take to that. So, this young punk coming down there. I'm going to go on that list, brother. You know, you mentioned that as you got out of farming here, that during the war you went to Portland, and you worked probably down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like, for you leaving and going down?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it was quite a different change for me, which was I went to school for a while, before I went down there, took some welding course, and I went down there and welding stuff, and stuff came, but it was a good change because we were in debt,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and I worked awful hard. I worked at the foundry, swing shift at the foundry, and then I worked at a garage,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: from nine o 'clock until the time I had to go to work down there at the garage, and so I burned in a can of wok in there until I had it, because I just couldn't take it. It was, it got out of debt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You held two jobs at once?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I had two jobs at once. So I worked on the swing shift, it gave me quite a little bit of time and away, and then it didn't give me too much sleep time either, so I worked with the two jobs there for, oh I guess I worked for near a month though, before I had to give up the garage job.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And when I first started at the foundry, I could work as many hours as I wanted to, and I worked there, and they finally cut me down there, just on one hour, and then I got this other job.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, when we talked about, well I asked you before about prejudice in the area here, I asked you before about prejudice around here, and you said that, well, that you would never notice it being local.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, well I'll tell you, it's a little different between, with the settle families, we were, we were from the rural district, you know, but as far as we know about Moscow, we never know, we didn't know much about city life until after we moved over here and started the school here, now that's the first time you know, but of course now, I know there's prejudice here alright, I know that black people have come here, and I was going, I have, I've talked to him here, what was he doing now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the guy that, was this the guy who had the boys working for him?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, yeah. Because you told me they went to the restaurant. Yeah, uh huh, yeah. Well see I was wondering about whether you run into that in Portland at all, whether you ever run into, in Portland, when you were working in Portland. Oh well, of course, no I didn't because, I was, I was, I boarded with my sister, but I never, I never ran into, I never ran into any of that, because I didn't, like I might, I didn't go no where, I was just going back and forth to work, and I didn't have, I mean, first, kind of socializing about anything, I didn't do anything, and so I never ran into any of it, because I didn't go no where to run into any of it. But you figure it was probably there in Portland at the time. Yeah, I'll say it was there in Portland. In fact, the matter, it is, it is, it didn't happen to me, but I just, from what I hear, and a lot of other people, it's pretty prevalent too, because that is the time there's shipyards there, and there's so many, there's so many blacks from the south, and other places coming out of work, you know. And then there's quite a lot of places just there. These places, you get in places, you get in places to stay and one thing or another, and then I think after a while they got,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think there's one hotel there, an old hotel, an older hotel there, I think. I think, I won't say for sure, but I believe it's, I don't know who did it, or how it came about, but anyway, you made it, what I mean, what I'm trying to say, who made it possible, but there's one hotel there that I think it is, it was, the blacks took it over, got it, and now what's going to happen is they've had lots of them staying there, and then they had these,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: like, theater camps in different places where they, I know I went out to visit a friend of mine out over, out of Swan Island shipyard there, and I'm a two before, it was like a trailer house, you could stay over there and then how for housing, they were white, they were living there,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and of course you see that they didn't,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and they didn't hire any black welders at the shipyards, of course I knew that before I went down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so I went, so I went down there, and then I went to the employment office,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and that's why I got on and got on at things, I just was lucky that,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: you didn't get in down at the foundry, but I didn't get in as a welder at the start down there, I went down and got another job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that, what did you start as?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I started, I was a crane man's helper, well what I mean by that, they'd mow these big, cast these big pieces of steel for the ships, and they'd take the mold off of them, and then the fella would run in the crane, he'd pick them up and set them over, out of the way so the government could examine them for any flaws and that was my job, I was down on the ground and I hooked the chain, I hooked the chain on these molds to pull them up, set them over, that was the first job I had. I forget what you call them now, they don't call them a crane man's helper, I'm not sure what these others are, I worked at that, well I guess I must have worked at that, once or maybe more than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then there was an opening?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, well anyway, they burnt up a, they burnt up a furnace, and they burnt it up, and they burnt it up, and that's what they call them either, that's why they melt the iron anyway, and they had to crawl under them, and there's just about that much under there to weld it, and the fella that was welding, one of the fellas was welding, he had a welder there, he was trying to fix it, and it was hot, and he at that time was doing it, he was just tight, he was a big man too you know, and he just about passed out, and I was working the swing shift down there,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and of course I said the foreman, my foreman knew I was, I asked for welding jobs before I could get on as welder when I went there, and so he said, oh you know, he said, well do you think you can get under there and weld, can you weld?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I said well I can give it a try, and he said okay, get your gear, so I went and got my helmet, it was already down there, and I welded the darn thing, and it wasn't long after that, I don't know why, and the next day or two I don't know whether it was him or the boss, the foreman had any words or what, I don't know why, but in the next two or three days while this head welder, he blew up and quit, and I got the job, unless he was there only about two or three days after that, and he blew up and quit, and something happened, and I got the job,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: but I think though to start with, he was supposed to have something wrong with him, I think it was when the foreman came to me, and told me that I'd be welding, and I was to be welding for a few days, and I wanted to weld for a few days, so what's his name, got back, and I told him yeah, so I went in there, and of course I didn't think I'd only be there two or three days, so he got back, and he never did get back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then you became a head welder?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay for long with the job there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I stayed down there until he started talking about going on a strike, and I got a harvest leave to come home. Yeah, that's when you got the job working for the grain growers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you'd been there for how long?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I've been down just over a year down in Portland, and I never went back, but I had a good job to go back to if I went back, but just talking about it,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I stayed here, and good thing I did, I got this job with the grain growers. You didn't want to be guilty of striking in wartime. Yeah, so I put in a harvest, worked that pole in the harvest field here, I'm buying them a pole, after that I got a job with the grain growers, and so I stayed there, and I didn't go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the foundry and the shipyard were separate? Huh? The foundry and the shipyard group were separate?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they were separate, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this all private, or was it government?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, the government had control of the foundry, it was all government, so they had control of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the foundry was casting before the shipyard?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Before the shipyard, yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
