words: And they wouldn't let blocks weld in the shipyard?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, seemed like they had some trouble of some kind down there before I went down there, because I knew that I could get in the shipyard there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There's a welder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of course was it here that you got, that you learned to do the welding?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it's just a welding course, they had it over here where it was our store there, it used to be a kind of factory where I used to build a little old combine over there, and they had the big building over there, and they was teaching welding over there, and there was another fellow here in town, that I'd go down there at night and take welding lessons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was you working for?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was working for Washburn Wilson, the seed company here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I wonder if that's where, I think Helmer Ringsage told me, I don't know if you know him, but he lives on the edge of town there, he got some kind of training and he went down to Portland.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's probably where he went to welding, that's where he went to, a lot of the Washburn people went down there. So it was a real useful skill. Yes it was, it sure helped me out, I got down there and got this welding job, made some good money, got myself all of this, and got straightened out.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I'm going to have to go pretty quick, but I wanted, there's a couple things I want to ask you before I leave, and one of them is about what the old time Fourth of July's used to be like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering what the celebration used to be like, say like, did they used to have celebrations out at Linville for the Fourth?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, let me see now, we went down, yeah we went out there, but I went down to, I went out,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: no I don't believe I ever was, but I was right out here at Cornwall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At Cornwall?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Cornwall used to have a Fourth of July celebration out there, there used to be two stores and a blacksmith shop out there at Cornwall, and I went to a celebration there and they didn't have a workspace there, and then I went to one at Linville too, that was after I left the farm though, when I went down there to a celebration down there, I used to play on the ball game down there, not Linville, but up where this school I toured up, the coast of where I live, Aspen Hill, we used to play at Linville, we played on the ball game down there, and went down there after, I guess I was in high school. I played ball down there on the Fourth of July, and then they had horse races and picnics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that mostly, was horse races and picnics, was there speeches too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, there wasn't no speaker at all, they had foot races for the young people, and we had horse races, just coming kind of useless around.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The Linville guys played cornwall guys in the baseball game, and that's about all there was to it. Of course they had a big dance that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people stay out there instead of coming into Moscow?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, there was no celebration in Moscow, I mean, you know, that was July when it was out there. When they had a celebration in Moscow, it was, you know, the town didn't have no celebration, but when Moscow didn't celebrate, they not give them a chance to have their own little private celebration. Moscow used to have some big celebrations there, of course,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: some good ones. Of course, those were the horse days, and they used to have horse shows, you know, which was nice, you'd see them off their, you'd see farmers show off their horses.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they had horse races.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they always had, of course, they always had a big carnival here at that time too, so they used to have a big time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When I was a little kid, that's about the only time we'd get to come to Moscow was maybe on the 4th of July, or whenever they had a circus here. They had to bring us to town. We never knew much about city life until after we moved over here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So that's why my folks wouldn't hear to it, when my sister wanted to go to Spokane to get a job and go to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember hearing about Marcus Garvey? Marcus Garvey?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think he's the Back to Africa man who was, he was a brother.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I remember his name, he was a woman.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's why I think he did, he was big on that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He was a, I don't even know what part of the country he was operating out of. I don't remember even, I thought you mentioned it, I remember that he was a fella that was planning to encourage the blacks to go back to Africa. I think so. I think, I know what I remember reading or hearing about, there was a man, that was Marcus Garvey, I guess that was the name, I forgot the name. But that was, I know there was a man long years ago, when I was a little kid or before I was born, anyway there was a guy that was trying to, was a, he was trying to get blacks to, go back to Africa and colonize, but I don't know, Marcus Garvey, I guess that was the name. Well I don't see now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Who was the guy that, that did so much with the peanut?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Washington. Booker T.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Washington, right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, Booker T. Washington, he was, he built the first colored school. Right, right, that's right.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Was that Burbank? No, they did so much with the peanut, was that Marcus Garvey? No, that wasn't, that doesn't sound like his name either, but I wonder if you, what the deuce was he would name it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He did so much with the peanut, you know, the peanuts, with all this stuff. I ain't got something.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't think of it either. Well I was wondering if there were any figures that when you were growing up, any guys that were kind of heroes to the kids, and you and your brothers, say, I was thinking maybe Booker T. Washington or somebody like that might have been, if it was me I imagine, you know, it would have been Jack Johnson or Joe Lewis.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, now Booker T. Washington, now that's who my youngest brother was named after, and he named himself. Mother was cutting Booker T. Washington's picture out of a paper and telling the volume what he did, what he was doing, what he was doing, and then my brother, my little brother, he decided that to be his name, so that's the name he picked, and he named himself. Up until that time, his name was Babe, is the only name he had, was Babe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he was just a little kid at the time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My youngest brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was just a child?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was just about two or three years old. Of course, I know he never was named, he was just called Babe. Until Mother's cutting Booker T. Washington's picture out of a paper and she was talking about what the great man he did and what he did, and he took the name, and that's what his name was until he died, Booker T. When I started high school up here, I was only black in the school, high school, and they tried to pin that name on me, Booker T.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They started calling me Booker T, but they found out I had a brother in there, Booker T, so that didn't hang too good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever hear of that guy, Dubois? W -E -B, Dubois? Dubois?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, uh -huh. Dubois, yeah, he was quite a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what would you call him, a radical?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't think of what... Anyway, he was quite a... Anyway, he was a man that outspoken on... I'm like the fellow at the peanut now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I know he was noted as a real good thinker. Yeah, he had a pretty good brain, but he was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't he kind of a left -winger?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was. That's what I was thinking of. He seemed like he was kind of off -center there. Well, I was outspoken about him. But he was a pretty smart cookie, I knew that. But he was kind of... He was quite a ways to the left.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, MacGarver, he's the guy that gets the peanut. Yeah, MacGarver, I think he got the peanut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were raising your daughter, when she was just a kid and growing up, did you ever try to explain to her the black -white relations and the prejudice and how that operated?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I imagine you must have talked to her about it some, because you told me she never experienced it. No, I never... Of course, she had her own experiences, little experiences, but I don't think... I never did just sit down and try to tell her that she was black and so you better keep in your place and stuff like that. I never did preach that to her. Well, that would have been a pretty bad... Yeah, but that's just like my younger brother, you know, my Booker and I. He was a kid. He didn't know he was black.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he got away pretty good. He just... It seems like it never did bother him. He was the one, but it just didn't. Of course,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: us older kids...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, our parents were raised in the South.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they hadn't been away from there too long. When we came along, of course, we heard a lot about segregation and the wrongs and what the blacks... What kind of a treatment the blacks experienced and all of that. I was very much alive with my family when I was coming up, and I was very much in love with my family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For your daughter growing up, do you believe that she really didn't ever, ever have any...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: experience any prejudice until she left here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I know you said it was never bad. Oh, no. Well, she liked it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She liked it. It seems to me like that when she was a little kid and she started going to school here and going to school here in town, she liked it. I won't say, but she liked it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She ran in. She kind of ran into the Calabarri and some home here. I won't say for sure now. I'll have to see where my father was. She joined the Browning Scout Troop.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It seemed like there was some woman here in town

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: that more or less objected to it or something. I don't remember who it was. Anyway,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: she was... They met at her home, or she was at this home, and my daughter, her little daughter, she was pretty upset about it. She told her mother about it. That's the only time I ever heard her complain. I don't just remember what that was, but she had joined the Browning Troop. It seemed like some kind of party or something at some lady's house. Somebody made some kind of remark to let her know that she was black. I forget what it was now, but anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that for her then, that her consciousness of being black and black culture has grown a lot after she's been an adult and been down in Berkeley and California? Or do you think she had it when she was growing up?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know. Now you take it to Elle. Now you take it to Corse. Of course, now when she first here and went to college, she said that when she finished her eighth grade, that she wanted to finish college and work for two years, and then she would think about getting married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She married on her junior and sophomore year here in Moscow. She got married, and she married a white boy. He was from, I guess I've told you this before, he was from Pennsylvania. Pennsylvania Dutch, I guess.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They went together here their junior year, and he graduated, and he was there in mining. He worked up here in the mines. He was there during the summer months. So after he graduated here, he was going to go to school, he was going to take further study over at WSU. And of course they married, and he was up in the mines, and she went up there and lived there in the mines that summer. He came back down first September just before school started to go to Pullman to register his own, and he got over there while they had dropped the course that he wanted, see. And there wasn't enough interest, they told him there wasn't enough interest in it, so they just discontinued it. And so he said, well, nothing to do now but go back home. So they went back to Pennsylvania, and he went to Penn State. And my daughter went back then, she went to Penn State. She put in her junior year at Penn State, her father was senior year I guess at Penn State.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And it was a good experience for her, she got to go to a big, large college, which she found out was a whole lot different from going to Idaho.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then after they finished, she came back there with us, and that was the idea now, and she wanted to get married. I tried to talk to them about it, I talked to them about it, I didn't know anything they should.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But they would probably find one with a different barrier and things that wouldn't be so good for them. And I said, well, on top of that, I didn't get a chance to get my degree, but I said I want most to finish college. We're going to get her a degree. Well, they talked that over too, because when he got to school, and he'd get a job, and it's in Lewiston School, so she said, well, get her a degree. Get her a degree, well, so I set up. So it turned out the way that they had to go to Pennsylvania to go to school like that, so she went to school, and she tried to work, and go to school to help out, because there wasn't much money, and he didn't have much coming, and it is dependent on us, but she couldn't do it. She found out it took all her time for a book, so she had to give it up. Anyway, after he got his degree, they got a job in Lewiston, I remember correctly now, it was somewhere in Kansas.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then they worked there for a while, about six months or maybe quite a year, I don't know if you all know that. They was thinking about buying a home there, but they didn't. And then they kept this job, this big copper mining company down here, Kenny Cox or something like that. Anyway, they got a job out here at Salt Lake. They had a couple of mining companies, so they moved to Salt Lake. And then, a year after they moved to Salt Lake, my granddaughter was born, and just about time she was born, he got a job down here in Arizona. They sent him down to Arizona, and of course we got to Arizona, there was no place out there in the desert to move a family.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: they stored us this. They decided to store the furniture at Salt Lake City, and Lois would come on up home and stay with us until they got, I don't know if they was going to build some housing down at the mine until he got a sufficient place for them. Well, she came home, she had a little baby come on home here, and she got here just just before summer school started. And she said, as long as she's going to be here, she just felt to go to summer school, so we paid her tuition, and she would drive back and forth. And she went to start the summer school, well that was alright with her husband, it was alright, I suppose that wouldn't last long. And then after she got through summer school,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't just remember now how it happened, I think she did that summer school, I don't believe it, I don't believe it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did she finish up?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I guess she finished.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I mean, anyway, it was the next year that she finished. I won't say it anyway, but I know that he came up to her graduation.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So she stayed for a while and kept going to school probably. Uh huh.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then after her graduation, she was just about ready for, she got a job here in Moscow as a student teacher for training up here in Moscow. And I guess that's when, that's when he got ready. He wanted her to come on home, come on down there and stay out there. And she told her no, she said she was going to finish up, as long as she's going this far. So that was in the agreement anyway, that she would finish up. And he got pretty hostile and he wrote to her and he called and he called and she called and so she... And she kept insisting that she wanted to finish up so she could teach if she had to, because she couldn't, like you and... And he got tired of it. So he... He called her up one time and... So he got, man, you know, his high horse. He told her well and said, if you want to stay up there, well, you just want to get her the horse.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And... She made up her mind, well, she said, I'll just do that. I'm through college now and I've got to where I can teach and I'm taking one way in. So she went, she just went down the path of the horse and she had her pigeon, so... Then... Boy, and... So when she and he got... He got the notice and going, so he turned around and he was here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But she wouldn't change her mind. She said, no, you asked for it. So I'm going to give it to you. And so they, I'll give her a day or two. I called Claire for a drive and... She wouldn't give in until she went on and got her... She went and got her job down and she went to California and got her job teaching.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he came over there and he told her if she wanted to teach, he'd come to California and go to Cal to summer school and take up something so that he could teach too.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Anyway, he made all kinds of operations, but they didn't. He didn't change his mind.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So... He taught for ten years there and then she got her job at college. She got her out of there to be ready. She's working on her doctorate now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She is now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. She kind of got her to master, so she didn't stop since she kept going. She said, try for a doctorate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever remarry? Huh? Did she ever remarry?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Not yet. No.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's a really good girl. And so what I'm going to say now, she's telling her mother here, she said, summer, summer, summer, or there's something to her. And her upsell, he finally got married again, but he wanted to... She said she had told him that

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: only counted her lesbets sick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said why not?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: If she had picked him back, he would divorce the woman he married. She thought that. She kind of laughed that off. Well, you must have really liked her. Yeah. Power around like that. Yeah, you did. You thought that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that you were right that they had trouble because of the color difference?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it wasn't the color difference. It wasn't that at all. No, it wasn't the color difference. It didn't sound like it. No, it wasn't the color difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you were telling them not to get married, was that what you were thinking, or was it just the college?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I was thinking more of the college because I felt this way. If they wanted to take the chance on that and they thought they could get along, I figured that was their privilege. That's the way I felt and feel about that. I feel for myself why I wouldn't want to marry out of my color. But I feel if a man did or a woman did, if they want to do that, I figure that's their privilege. Of course, they got certain other barriers to reach. Of course, they ought to be old enough to understand that. I figured that if they wanted to, well, that's their privilege. So your objection was to the fact that she had these ties. No, I wanted her to have an education. That's what I wanted to do because I hadn't lost out on mine. And I wanted her to go ahead and finish school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And she finished and it's a good thing she did. Because I tell you right now, she had to put her up front. She hadn't had a college degree so she could teach.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was in the Methodist Church. She's a little kid. She and some of the other guys, they left their church, whatever it was they belonged to. And they joined a Unitarian church.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Their segregation policy, they didn't have anything. I can't explain what that was. I didn't. It wasn't much, but I know that they did that much. They did a long while at Marshall Luther King these times. But other side, other than that,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't think that, to my knowledge anyway, that she never belonged to anything else. Well, you had told me that she saw things really differently after she left. Yeah, she did. That's right. She changed. She saw different things. And she was very outspoken.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But her scores

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: didn't do anything.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know really and I don't think she did. But I know that, whether she joined any of the other organizations. Working for Marshall Luther King or not, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Gene, I should go. I've stayed a long time. It's getting on towards dinner here. Well, Sam, my son George is glad you came over. I really enjoyed you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I really enjoyed coming and seeing you again.
